front: Film: a poor family infiltrates the wealthy Park household by each posing as an unrelated professional; two siblings rehearse the jingle 'Jessica, only child, Illinois, Chicago' before ringing the doorbell
Film: the Parks' housekeeper is forced out by exploiting her peach fuzz allergy; an aria from Handel's Rodelinda plays over the montage of the Kims getting rid of her
Film: a man secretly lives in the Parks' underground bunker and flickers a stairway light to communicate with his wife; a chauffeur dresses as a Native American chief at a birthday party
Film: first non-English film to win the Academy Award for Best Picture; director mentioned the 'one-inch tall barrier of subtitles' in his Oscar speech
Film: ends with a former detective peering down a farm drainpipe, then staring directly into the camera after a girl describes an ordinary man who returned to the crime scene
Film: the serial killer targets women wearing red on rainy nights; a pop song plays on the radio each time, and a policewoman discovers the radio station connection

Film: a giant mutant creature (nicknamed 'Steve Buscemi' by the crew) uses its tail to snatch a narcoleptic man's daughter near the Han River; inspired by the McFarland incident and US Army 'Agent Yellow'
Film: opens with an old woman dancing in a wheat field; its best-known song is 'The Belt of Faith'

Film: Tilda Swinton's character tells poor tail-section passengers to 'be the shoe' and freezes the arm of someone who threw a shoe at her; set on a perpetually circling train after a climate catastrophe
Director: became the first Korean director to win the Palme d'Or at Cannes; his films frequently star Song Kang-ho
